title_en: The Author and His Purpose: Ibn al-Jawzi's Abridgement of the Ihya
title_ar: المؤلف وهدفه: اختصار ابن الجوزي للإحياء
content_en:
Imam Abu al-Faraj Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Jawzi (510–597 AH / 1116–1200 CE) was one of the most prolific and influential scholars in Islamic history. A Hanbali scholar based in Baghdad, he wrote hundreds of works spanning Quranic exegesis, hadith sciences, history, biography, medicine, ethics, and preaching. He was renowned as a master preacher whose sessions drew enormous crowds, and his works on Islamic spirituality, ethics, and the diseases of the heart are among the most practically oriented in the tradition.

Minhaj al-Qasidin wa-Mufid al-Sadiqin (The Path of the Seekers and the Benefit of the Sincere) is his abridgement and revision of Imam al-Ghazali's Ihya Ulum al-Din (The Revival of the Religious Sciences). The Ihya, composed by Abu Hamid al-Ghazali (d. 505 AH / 1111 CE) in the late fifth Islamic century, is one of the most comprehensive works on Islamic spirituality ever written — a four-volume encyclopedic treatment of worship, social conduct, destructive traits of the soul, and the path to salvation. It became one of the most widely read books in Islamic history.

However, Imam al-Ghazali's Ihya contains significant weaknesses that serious scholars identified: numerous hadith that are fabricated, extremely weak, or undocumented; elements of Sufi thought and spiritual practice that are unsupported by the Quran and authentic Sunnah; and occasionally philosophical influences that are difficult to reconcile with the transmitted scholarly consensus. The hadith problem was documented most famously by the hafiz Zayn al-Din al-Iraqi (d. 806 AH), who wrote a complete hadith verification study of the Ihya, noting the status of every narration — authentic, weak, or fabricated.

Ibn al-Jawzi's Minhaj al-Qasidin addresses these weaknesses directly. He took the Ihya's essential structure — its four sections on worship, social conduct, destructive qualities, and the path to God — and rebuilt it on a foundation of authentic hadith. Narrations that are fabricated or extremely weak are removed. Sufi practices and beliefs unsupported by the authentic Sunnah and the scholarly consensus of the Salaf are either removed or explicitly noted as such. What remains is the spiritual and ethical content of the Ihya purified of its weaknesses: a practical guide to Islamic character formation, inner purification, and the path to God grounded in what the Prophet ﷺ and the Companions actually taught.

This makes Minhaj al-Qasidin a superior practical choice for most students of Islamic spirituality. It preserves the organizing wisdom of the Ihya while removing the elements that require the reader to have advanced hadith knowledge to navigate safely. The work is, in Ibn al-Jawzi's own framing, a service to Muslims who want the Ihya's guidance without the danger of inadvertently acting on fabricated reports attributed to the Prophet ﷺ.
